senses.definition: Plural of aboideau: small water-control dams with gates used to keep seawater out of marshland while letting river water drain away.
examples: Historic aboideaus still protect the reclaimed marshes along parts of the Canadian coast.
Engineers inspected the old aboideaus to make sure the gates still closed properly at high tide.

senses.definition: Alternative plural of aboideau: small tidal dams with gates that let freshwater out of marshes but stop seawater coming in.
examples: The old aboideaux along the river are important Acadian heritage structures.
Maintenance of the aboideaux is essential for protecting the farmland from saltwater.

senses.definition: Boiling or full of boiling liquid.
examples: The pot was aboil when she threw in the pasta.
We waited until the water was fully aboil before adding the vegetables.

senses.definition: Very excited or full of strong activity or emotion.
examples: Rumours set the whole town aboil during the trial.
Social media was aboil with comments after the announcement.

senses.definition: A dike or dam with a gate that lets freshwater drain from farmland at low tide but blocks seawater at high tide, used to protect marshland.
examples: The farmers built an aboiteau across the creek to reclaim the salt marsh.
At low tide, the aboiteau opens to release water from the fields.

senses.definition: Plural of aboiteau: dikes or tidal dams with gates that protect low-lying farmland from seawater.
examples: Several aboiteaus were constructed along the estuary in the eighteenth century.
The old aboiteaus still show how Acadian settlers managed the tides.

senses.definition: Alternative plural of aboiteau: tidal dikes with gates used to drain marshland and keep out seawater.
examples: The aboiteaux along the bay transformed marshes into rich farmland.
Many of the original aboiteaux have been replaced by modern concrete structures.

senses.definition: An extremely rare or obsolete word with no common modern use.
examples: The term "abolete" appears only in historical word lists and is not used in current English.
He found the mysterious word "abolete" in an old glossary.

senses.definition: To officially end a law, system, or practice, especially by a government or authority.
examples: The government plans to abolish the old tax on imports.
Slavery was abolished in many countries in the nineteenth century.
They are campaigning to abolish the death penalty.

senses.definition: That can be ended or done away with, especially by a law or decision.
examples: Some experts argue that the practice is harmful but still abolishable.
Not all traditional customs are abolishable, even if they seem outdated.

senses.definition: Ended officially, especially by a law or authority (past tense and past participle of abolish).
examples: Child labour was officially abolished in that region decades ago.
The outdated regulation was abolished after public pressure.

senses.definition: A person or group that ends a law, system, or practice.
examples: History remembers him as an abolisher of unjust laws.
Each abolisher of a harmful custom faced strong resistance.

senses.definition: People or groups that bring an end to laws, systems, or practices.
examples: The abolishers of the old regime were later praised as heroes.
Many abolishers of cruel traditions worked quietly at the local level.

senses.definition: Ends something officially, especially a law or system (third person singular of abolish).
examples: The new bill abolishes several outdated regulations.
This reform effectively abolishes the old grading system.

senses.definition: Ending something officially, especially a law or system (present participle of abolish).
examples: They are discussing abolishing the requirement for military service.
Abolishing the fees would make education more accessible.

senses.definition: The act of officially ending a law, system, or practice.
examples: The abolishment of the old policy was welcomed by students.
Many activists have called for the abolishment of nuclear weapons.

senses.definition: Acts of officially ending laws, systems, or practices.
examples: These reforms include several important abolishments of unfair regulations.
Over time, a series of abolishments changed the legal system completely.

senses.definition: The official ending of a law, system, or practice, especially something seen as wrong or unfair.
examples: The abolition of slavery was a turning point in history.
Many people support the abolition of the death penalty.
Tax abolition in certain sectors helped small businesses.

senses.definition: Relating to the ending or abolishing of a law, system, or practice.
examples: He wrote an abolitional critique of the current prison system.
Abolitional efforts focused on removing discriminatory laws.

senses.definition: Aiming at or supporting the abolition of a law, system, or practice.
examples: The group adopted an abolitionary position on capital punishment.
Her abolitionary views made her unpopular with some officials.

senses.definition: To make something abolitionist in character or to bring about its abolition (British spelling, rare).
examples: Some activists sought to abolitionise the legal system of the colony.
The movement tried to abolitionise existing institutions of punishment.

senses.definition: Made abolitionist in character or brought to an end, especially in a legal or political context (past tense of abolitionise, rare).
examples: The old system was gradually abolitionised by a series of reforms.
Once fully abolitionised, the institution no longer used corporal punishment.

senses.definition: Making something abolitionist in character or working to end it (present participle of abolitionise, rare).
examples: They were abolitionising the old structures step by step.
Abolitionising the prison system requires deep social change.

senses.definition: The belief in or movement for ending a law, system, or practice, especially slavery.
examples: Abolitionism became a powerful force in the nineteenth century.
Modern prison abolitionism questions the very idea of prisons.

senses.definition: Different forms or types of abolitionist belief or movements.
examples: Scholars compare various abolitionisms that arose in different countries.
Contemporary abolitionisms focus on issues beyond slavery, such as prisons and policing.

senses.definition: A person who wants to end a law, system, or practice, especially someone who worked to end slavery.
examples: The abolitionist risked his life to help enslaved people escape.
She describes herself as a prison abolitionist.

senses.definition: Wanting or supporting the ending of a law, system, or practice.
examples: They formed an abolitionist group to oppose capital punishment.
Abolitionist ideas spread quickly through the region.

senses.definition: People who want to end a law, system, or practice, especially those who worked to end slavery.
examples: Abolitionists organized secret routes to help enslaved people escape.
Modern abolitionists question the role of prisons in society.

senses.definition: To make something abolitionist in character or to bring about its abolition (US spelling, rare).
examples: They hoped to abolitionize the institution of slavery completely.
The activists worked to abolitionize harsh forms of punishment.

senses.definition: Made abolitionist in character or brought to an end, especially in a legal or political context (past tense of abolitionize, rare).
examples: The outdated system was eventually abolitionized through a series of reforms.
Several practices were quietly abolitionized over the following decade.

senses.definition: Making something abolitionist in character or working to end it (present participle of abolitionize, rare).
examples: By abolitionizing the institution, they hoped to remove its most harmful features.
The organization is abolitionizing outdated forms of punishment.

senses.definition: Acts of officially ending laws, systems, or practices.
examples: The nineteenth century saw many abolitions of unfair taxes.
Recent abolitions of outdated rules have simplified the legal code.

senses.definition: A heavy wool cloak worn in ancient Rome, especially by soldiers or philosophers.
examples: The statue shows a philosopher wrapped in an abolla.
In the painting, the general's abolla is thrown over one shoulder.

senses.definition: Plural of abolla: heavy wool cloaks worn in ancient Rome.
examples: The museum displays several reconstructed abollae from the Roman period.
In the scene, the soldiers throw their abollae over their armour.

senses.definition: Plural of abolla: heavy wool cloaks worn in ancient Rome.
examples: The actors put on abollas to recreate the look of Roman soldiers.
Scholars debate how colourful Roman abollas might have been.

senses.definition: A large non-poisonous snake of tropical America, such as the boa constrictor (rare, chiefly zoological).
examples: The explorers reported seeing an enormous aboma near the river.
An aboma lay coiled on a branch above the path.

senses.definition: Plural of aboma: large non-poisonous snakes of tropical America (rare).
examples: The forest was said to be home to giant abomas.
In the story, two abomas guard the entrance to the cave.

senses.definition: Plural of abomasum: the fourth stomach compartments of ruminant animals where most digestion happens.
examples: In calves, the abomasa are relatively large compared to the other stomachs.
The vet examined the abomasa for signs of disease.

senses.definition: Relating to the abomasum, the fourth stomach of ruminant animals.
examples: The cow suffered from abomasal displacement after calving.
Abomasal ulcers can be serious in young calves.

senses.definition: An alternative plural form of abomasum: fourth stomach compartments of ruminant animals (rare).
examples: The pathologist opened the abomasi to check for parasites.
Inflammation was found in several abomasi from the herd.

senses.definition: The fourth and final stomach compartment in ruminant animals, where true digestion with acid and enzymes takes place.
examples: The abomasum of the cow was examined for signs of infection.
In young calves, milk passes directly into the abomasum.

senses.definition: A very rare or non-standard form related to abomasum, not used in normal modern English.
examples: The form "abomasus" occasionally appears in older technical texts.
Most modern writers avoid "abomasus" and use "abomasum" instead.

senses.definition: An extremely rare or possibly erroneous plural form related to abomasum, not used in standard English.
examples: The spelling "abomasusi" is not accepted in modern veterinary writing.
Dictionaries do not usually list "abomasusi" as a correct plural.

senses.definition: The quality of being extremely unpleasant or very bad.
examples: Critics wrote about the sheer abominability of the conditions in the camp.
The abominability of the crime shocked the whole nation.

senses.definition: Very bad, unpleasant, or morally wrong.
examples: They lived in abominable conditions without clean water.
It was an abominable crime that shocked the community.
The food was abominable and almost impossible to eat.

senses.definition: The state of being extremely unpleasant, bad, or morally wrong.
examples: The abominableness of the regime became clear through witness reports.
She described the abominableness of the living conditions in detail.

senses.definition: In a very bad, unpleasant, or morally wrong way.
examples: The prisoners were treated abominably.
He behaved abominably towards his colleagues.
The room was abominably cold and damp.

senses.definition: To hate something very much and think it is extremely unpleasant or wrong.
examples: She abominates cruelty to animals.
They abominate any form of discrimination.

senses.definition: Hated very much and thought to be extremely unpleasant or wrong (past tense and past participle of abominate).
examples: They abominated the brutal regime that ruled their country.
Many people in the village abominated the idea of abandoning their traditions.

senses.definition: Hates something very much and thinks it is extremely unpleasant or wrong (third person singular of abominate).
examples: He abominates any form of bullying.
She openly abominates corruption in public office.

senses.definition: Hating something very much and thinking it is extremely unpleasant or wrong (present participle of abominate).
examples: Abominating all forms of injustice, she devoted her life to human rights.
They left the group, abominating its violent methods.

senses.definition: Something that is extremely unpleasant or very wrong and makes people feel strong dislike or shock.
examples: For many people, slavery is an absolute abomination.
The polluted river was described as an environmental abomination.
Such cruelty to animals is an abomination in any society.
